3 min read

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you are learning JavaScript, you might wonder what happens when your code actually runs. We learn best when we practice first and look at the theory later, so let us start right away with a simple piece of code and then figure out how it works behind the scenes.
var a = 2;
var b = 4;
function multiply(num1, num2) {
var result = num1 * num2;
return result;
}
var total = multiply(a, b);What does JavaScript do when it sees this code? It creates a massive invisible container called the Global Execution Context. You can think of this as the main workspace where everything in your file happens.
Inside this Global Execution Context, JavaScript always processes your code in two easy steps: a Memory Phase and an Execution Phase.
Step 1: The Global Memory Phase
Before JavaScript does any math, it quickly scans your file from top to bottom. It just wants to find your variables and functions to reserve space for them.
For variables, it does not know the real value yet. It puts in a temporary placeholder called undefined. For functions, it stores the entire block of code so it can use it later.
If we looked inside the computer memory during this phase, it would look like this:
// GLOBAL MEMORY PHASE
a: undefined
b: undefined
multiply: { var result = num1 * num2; return result; }
total: undefinedStep 2: The Global Execution Phase
Now that memory is ready, JavaScript goes back to the top of your file. It reads your code line by line and does the actual work.

• Line 1: It updates the memory for a to the number 2.

• Line 2: It updates the memory for b to the number 4.

• Lines 4 to 7: It skips the function for now because it already saved it in memory.
Now the main workspace looks like this:
// GLOBAL EXECUTION PHASE (Before the function runs)
a: 2
b: 4
multiply: { var result = num1 * num2; return result; }
total: undefinedNotice that total is still undefined. This is because we need to run the function to get the final answer.
Step 3: The Function Execution Context and Call Stack
When JavaScript gets to the last line var total = multiply(a, b);, it calls the function.
This triggers a very important rule. Whenever a function runs, JavaScript creates a brand new, smaller workspace just for that function. We call this the Function Execution Context.
To keep track of all these workspaces, JavaScript uses a system called the Call Stack. You can imagine this as a stack of heavy boxes. The big Global Execution Context is the box at the bottom. When the multiply function runs, its new Function Execution Context is placed right on top.
Inside this new mini workspace, JavaScript repeats the exact same two phases!
Mini Memory Phase
It creates memory for the inputs and the variables inside the function.
// MINI MEMORY PHASE (Inside the multiply function)
num1: undefined
num2: undefined
result: undefinedMini Execution Phase
Next, it runs the code inside the function.

• It assigns 2 to num1 and 4 to num2.

• It multiplies them, so result becomes 8.
// MINI EXECUTION PHASE (Inside the multiply function)
num1: 2
num2: 4
result: 8Step 4: Returning and Destroying
The function finishes with return result;. This tells the mini workspace to hand its final answer of 8 back down to the main workspace.
Once the answer is handed back, that Function Execution Context is completely destroyed. It is removed from the Call Stack to save memory.
Back in the Global Execution Context, the total variable finally gets its real value.
// FINAL GLOBAL MEMORY
a: 2
b: 4
multiply: { var result = num1 * num2; return result; }
total: 8Summary
That is exactly how JavaScript runs under the hood. It starts with a Global Execution Context. It uses a Memory Phase to set variables to undefined, and an Execution Phase to assign real values. Whenever it sees a function, it stacks a new Function Execution Context on the Call Stack. Understanding this structure makes writing and reading JavaScript a lot easier!
